sections.title: Connect & fund — a client and a BalanceManager
sections.key_moment: `setupWithBalanceManager()` returns the funded `{ client, keypair, manifest }` plus an on-chain `BalanceManager` — and the BM is not optional ceremony here. The shared sandbox `DEEP_SUI` vault's lendable `base_balance` fluctuates with the live market maker and can dip below the borrow amount (`ENotEnoughBaseForLoan`); `seedPoolBaseLiquidity(ctx, 5)` deposits 5 DEEP through the BM right before each borrow so the loan reliably has liquidity to draw. This is a sandbox-determinism crutch — production pools hold standing liquidity and real integrations skip it.

sections.title: The hot potato — a value the compiler won't let you drop
sections.key_moment: `deepbook::vault::FlashLoan` has NO abilities — no `drop`, no `store`, no `key`. `borrow_flashloan_base` hands it back alongside the borrowed coin, and the type system makes it impossible to discard, save, or transfer. The only sink is `pool::return_flashloan_base`, which destructures and consumes it. If the PTB ends with the potato alive, the *entire transaction aborts* — that single compile-time fact is what makes the loan risk-free for LPs: principal returns in full within the atomic tx, or nothing happened at all.

sections.title: Consume it — execute_base merges, repays exactly, settles the rest
sections.key_moment: Inside `execute_base`: `topup.join(borrowed)` merges the loan into the caller's coin, `assert!(topup.value() >= borrow_amount, ERepayShort)` guards the repay, `topup.split(borrow_amount, ctx)` peels off *exactly* the principal, and `pool::return_flashloan_base(pool, repay, loan)` consumes the potato. The vault asserts the returned coin's value EQUALS the principal exactly (`EIncorrectQuantityReturned`) — these loans carry no interest, so over-repaying aborts too. The repayment must be exact, not merely sufficient.

sections.title: Publish — stage the package into the sandbox and write deployment.json
sections.key_moment: `contracts/scripts/deploy.sh` stages `arb_executor.move` *inside* `$DEEPBOOK_SANDBOX_DIR/packages/` so its relative `token`/`deepbook` deps resolve, generates a `Move.toml` with NO `[addresses]` block (a named-address module binds its address via the `localnet` environment at publish time — an explicit block makes the build reject `localnet`), runs `sui client test-publish -e localnet`, and writes the published `arbExecutorPackageId` to `deployment.json`. That id is chain-specific — re-genesis the sandbox and you must re-publish, or the live test runs against a stale package.

sections.title: Assemble the PTB — borrow and settle in one atomic transaction
sections.key_moment: `const [coin, flashLoan] = tx.add(client.deepbook.flashLoans.borrowBaseAsset('DEEP_SUI', borrow))` and the `tx.moveCall` to `arb_executor::execute_base` live in the SAME `Transaction` — that is what makes borrow and repay atomic. The borrow thunk yields the borrowed `coin` and the `flashLoan` potato as NestedResults that flow directly into the move call's arguments; both are consumed before the tx ends. `borrowBaseAsset` takes a human amount and scales it internally, but the `borrow_amount` u64 the module must repay exactly is the *scaled* value (DEEP 1e6) — so the driver scales it the same way.

sections.title: Drive it & prove atomicity — the live revert gate
sections.key_moment: The negative test demands repayment LARGER than was borrowed (`overrideBorrowAmount: 1` on a `borrow: 0.5`, `topup: 0`) and asserts the abort message matches `/arb_executor::execute_base/` while NOT matching `/borrow_flashloan|ENotEnoughBaseForLoan/`. Asserting *where* the abort fired — inside your module's `ERepayShort` guard, not at the borrow step — is what proves the atomic unwind happened for the right reason: a liquidity shortfall (same code 1) would also throw, but only the real short-repay reverts the settled PTB. That distinction is the lesson: the loan unwinds completely or commits completely, never halfway.
